schema_version: v0 — migrates into the intention envelope when MAP.md Q6 lands
what: Intentions with no code to sit beside. The berth for anything whose implementation is NOT a directory in cairn/ — host-seams (the machine's own configuration) and concept-pieces (where the prose IS the implementation). Records here never migrate; a ticket that resolves becomes `history` in place.
why:
Akien, 2026-07-22: 'we'd have intentions-other and intentions... and intentions is built from intentions-other AND the intentions now homed in the code.' Law 5 says intent shares its implementation's address — which silently assumes every implementation HAS an address we own. Some do not. A hook in ~/.claude/settings.json, a package on the box, a widget in ~/.local/share: real built things, fulfilled intentions, and nowhere in cairn/ to be beside. Without this store they are simply lost, and losing them loses knowledge — which is the commons test, met exactly.

The sharper reason is the seed claim: the intentions folder is meant to be the thing you carry to a new machine and regrow the system from. A seed compiled only over the beside-code charters regrows a repo that does not RUN, because everything that made the machine ready to run it was homeless. This store is what makes 'take that one folder out and rebuild the whole thing' true rather than nearly true.
compiles_into: `intentions/` — the system-scale compiled view, projected over this store PLUS the charters homed beside the code. Outer row of the three-scale projector recursion (ticket: history -> state; component: intentions+tickets -> charter; system: both sources -> intentions/). Consequence, load-bearing and not yet enforced: once `intentions/` is compiled it must never be hand-edited (CLAUDE.md, 'a compiled view is never hand-edited'). It is hand-authored TODAY. Tracked as an IOU, not a resting state (Law 4).
owner: each record has exactly one owner who gates writes to it (Law 6). The store owns the collection; the emit chokepoint validates records against the template.

template.owner: the one owner who gates writes to this node
template.intention: what this node is FOR (the WHAT), present tense
template.state: the pipeline instance — where this node is in its class's gate-set
template.gates: the class's gate-set bound at casting
template.apply: host-seam ONLY, required — the replayable recipe. Complete enough that a bare machine reaches this state from this block alone, with nothing else from the repo. A node that merely describes its change is documentation, and documentation does not cast.
template.verify: host-seam ONLY, required — a re-runnable check that measures whether the claim is STILL true, plus what green looks like.
template.falsifier: what would show this node wrong (required to cast)
template.horizon: when to re-check — never optional for a host-seam, whose seal expires by nature

notes_on_the_store: A host-seam's seal EXPIRES. The host drifts with nothing in git changing, so a green VALIDATION here is a measurement with a date on it, not a permanent fact (Law 3).

Failing loudly is the hard part. Host-config scripts conventionally fail silent (exit 0, keep the last good value) so they don't break the desktop — which means a dead node looks exactly like a healthy one. Re-verify is the only thing standing between those two states.

The store is expected to stay SMALL and grow only against real need. One member at founding (usage-widget-hook). If it starts collecting every command ever run on the box, the filter has failed: a record earns its place by being something you would have to re-derive on a new machine.
provenance: Founded 2026-07-22, the same afternoon the usage-widget hook was built without an intention. Akien named both the gap and the store. Demanded, not pre-seeded.
